coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as an eco-friendly activator of sulphur vulcanization in diene-rubber composites
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Siwu
Xiao, Chong
Kong, Shaoxin
Li, Bo
Yang, Zhijun
Tang, Zhenghai
Liu, Fang
Guo, Baochun
coredata.dc:description: It is widely accepted that zinc oxide (ZnO) is the best activator for sulphur vulcanization of rubber composites. However, ZnO is recognized as a toxic substance for both human and aquatic organisms. Therefore, it is of great importance, but still challengeable to exploit eco-friendly alternatives to reduce ZnO, without comprising the performance of rubber composites. Herein, we describe a novel strategy to achieve substantial reduction of ZnO in diene-rubber composites, by utilizing carbon nanodots (CDs) as an efficient activator for sulphur vulcanization. With the substitution of CDs for ZnO, the cross-linking rate of the rubber composites remarkably accelerated, mainly due to the fact that the N-related moieties of CDs can facilitate the formation of sulphur-based networks via significantly reducing the activation energy of the cross-linking reactions. Moreover, the activation efficiency of the synthesized CDs was also confirmed in the rubber formulations filled with conventional fillers. We envisage that this strategy can provide a green and practical avenue towards the substantial pollution abatement from ZnO in rubber composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Activator
Zinc oxide
Sulphur vulcanization
Diene rubber

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of CDs as activator on accelerated sulphur cross-linking
Mechanism of accelerated sulphur cross-linking activated by CDs
Application of CDs as activator in nano-filled rubber formulations

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Cross-linking kinetics of ZxCy compounds at 150 °C; (b) normalized properties of ZxCy samples (EBA: elongation at break; TS: tensile strength; TM: tensile modulus; CK: cross-linking density).

(a) Cross-linking kinetics of Z2C3 compounds at different temperatures; (b) Temperature dependence of the cross-linking rate k of ZxCy samples. The activation energy was calculated by fitting k with Arrhenius equation; (c) HPLC chromatograms of the reaction products of model compounds after reacting at 150 °C for different times; (d) SEM and the corresponding EDS mapping images of Z2C3 sample.
(a) Cross-linking kinetics of Z5C0 and Z2C3 compounds filled with conventional fillers at 150 °C; (b) Representative strain-stress curves of Z5C0 and Z2C3 samples filled with conventional fillers.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as an eco-friendly activator of sulphur vulcanization in diene-rubber composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Materials Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Materials Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and Its Composite Materials, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Changchun, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and Its Composite Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and its Composite Materials, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Changchun, 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is widely accepted that zinc oxide (ZnO) is the best activator for sulphur vulcanization of rubber composites. However, ZnO is recognized as a toxic substance for both human and aquatic organisms. Therefore, it is of great importance, but still challengeable to exploit eco-friendly alternatives to reduce ZnO, without comprising the performance of rubber composites. Herein, we describe a novel strategy to achieve substantial reduction of ZnO in diene-rubber composites, by utilizing carbon nanodots (CDs) as an efficient activator for sulphur vulcanization. With the substitution of CDs for ZnO, the cross-linking rate of the rubber composites remarkably accelerated, mainly due to the fact that the N-related moieties of CDs can facilitate the formation of sulphur-based networks via significantly reducing the activation energy of the cross-linking reactions. Moreover, the activation efficiency of the synthesized CDs was also confirmed in the rubber formulations filled with conventional fillers. We envisage that this strategy can provide a green and practical avenue towards the substantial pollution abatement from ZnO in rubber composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Diene-rubber is one of the most widespread elastomers due to its indispensable applications in tire, seal and shock absorption. This strategically important material could hardly seek out any valuable applications until Charles Goodyear invented the seminal sulphur vulcanization process in 1839. The incorporation of sulphur into diene-rubber system can transform the linear rubber skeletons into a three-dimensional framework with a sulphur-bridged network at elevated temperatures, which have revolutionized the performance of the material with unique high elasticity, dimensional stability and mechanical robustness. After years of development, it is generally accepted that a consolidated formulation of diene-rubber should involve several phr (parts per hundred parts of rubber) inorganic activator for efficient vulcanization. Nowadays, zinc oxide (ZnO) is widely regarded as the best activator by significantly promoting the cross-linking efficiency of compound and optimizing the mechanical properties of products [1].

Zinc is one of the most common metallic elements in the earth, and also an indispensable element for human body. However, the anthropogenic increase of zinc above a critical concentration has led to worldwide environmental concerns on the toxic effect of excess zinc. According to the “Material Safety Data Sheet”, ZnO is classified as a moderately toxic chemical for human body, which can induce contact dermatitis and significant inflammation (or even irreversible pathology) of cardio-pulmonary system by repeated inhalation or ingestion [2]. Besides, ZnO is also adjudged to be very toxic to aquatic organism, may cause long-term adverse effects in the aquatic environment [3]. In view of these, the European Union had promulgated ordinances that the packaging of ZnO in the jurisdictions must be tagged “Environmentally Hazardous Substance”. The global annual production of ZnO reaches up to tens of million tons, about 50–60% of which is consumed in the rubber industry [4,5]. During production, use, reclamation, and disposal of rubber goods, thousands of ZnO certainly will leak into the environment, particularly through the rubber dusts worn form tyres [6]. It has been pointed out that the quantity of Zn released by tyre abrasions from vehicular traffic is positively associated with childhood asthma exacerbations [7].

Accordingly, from the environmental point of view, it is of great importance to seek eco-friendly and practical alternatives for ZnO, in order to reduce the zinc content in rubber formulations. Hitherto, several plausible strategies have been proposed [8], involving the substitution of nano-sized ZnO [9–11], other metal oxides [12,13], zinc-containing nanoparticles [14,15] or amine complexes [16] for ZnO. However, most of these substitutes are still inherently toxic [17,18] and their preparations should generally involve time-consuming and multi-step reactions [19,20], which severely suppress their commercial application. Recently, Glebova et al. described that nitrogen-doped graphene (N-GE) can partially substitute ZnO to activate the sulphur cross-linking reactions in diene-rubber systems [21]. The authors phenomenally attributed the results to the incorporated N-GE can enable an accelerated decomposition of organic accelerators to form initial radicals for cross-linking. Similar results have also been observed in graphene oxide filled rubbers [22]. Despite its efficient activation, it is known that the manufacturing and doping processes of N-GE are also quite complicated and high-cost [23]. However, this enlightening work still reminds us of another fascinating carbogenic nanoparticle, carbon nanodots (CDs). CDs, a newly rising star in the family of nano-carbon materials, have drawn intensive interest due to their outstanding photoluminescence, excellent electron donating/accepting ability, high chemical inertness and biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and abundant low-cost sources [24,25]. Therefore, these quasi-spherical nanoparticles exhibit a tremendous potential in a myriad of applications in diverse fields, including optoelectronics, energy, biomedicine, and polymer nanocomposites [26–28]. In addition, the surface functionality and inner core structure of CDs can be lightly and efficiently tuned by directly adding passivation agents to introduce heteroatoms in a one-step synthetic process. Hitherto, nitrogen is recognized to be the most effective dopants for CDs and a variety of N-containing compounds have been exploited to integrate N-related moieties into CDs [29,30]. In our previous studies, different amine-passivated CDs with tunable N-related moieties were synthesized via a facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis by utilizing kinds of diamines as surface passivation agents. We demonstrated that the incorporation of these synthesized CDs into diene-rubber systems can achieve significant improvement on the thermo-oxidative resistance and mechanical performance of the matrix [31,32].

By virtue of the abundant N-related moieties of amine-passivated CDs, in this work we report a novel and universal strategy to achieve substantial reduction of toxic ZnO in diene-rubber systems by utilizing this eco-friendly nanoparticle as an efficient activator for conventional sulphur vulcanization. For this purpose, amine-passivated CDs was incorporated into commercial styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) formulations to substitute equivalent ZnO. The results turned out the substitution of CDs for ZnO can remarkably reduce the activation energy of the sulphur cross-linking reactions, and hence facilitate the formation of sulphur-based networks in SBR composites. Even in the case that 60 wt% of ZnO was substituted by equivalent CDs, the mechanical performance and cross-linking density of the sample are identical to the unsubstituted one. In addition, the activation efficiency of the synthesized CDs on sulphur cross-linking process was also confirmed in the rubber formulations filled with conventional fillers.

Citric acid (CA, 99%), 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA, 98%), N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazolyl sulfenamide (CZ, ≥98%), dibutyl disulfide (DD, ≥98.5%), and 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene (TME, 98%) were purchased from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Styrene-butadiene rubber (Trademark: SBR1502, styrene content 23.5 wt%) was purchased from Jilin Chemical Industry Co., Jilin, China. Precipitated silica (Trademark: ULTRASIL 7000 GR) and carbon black (Trademark: VULCAN 3) were purchased from Qingdao Dexinyuan Industrial & Trading Co., Ltd., Qingdao, China. The other rubber additives were industrial grade and were used as received.

Amine-passivated CDs were prepared according to a facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis method reported elsewhere [32,33]. Briefly, 1.0 g of CA was dissolved in 10 mL of phosphate solution, and 0.313 g of EDA was dropwise added under mildly stirring. The solution was then heated with a household microwave at 700 W for 3 min, which turned into a red-brown solid. After cooling, the obtained solid was dissolved in distilled water and was washed with excess anhydrous ethanol several times to remove soluble byproducts and unreacted residues. The purified CDs were dried in vacuo at 50 ᵒC for further use.

A desired amount of amine-passivated CDs and curing ingredients were admixed with SBR in an internal mixer at room temperature. The resultant compounds were subjected to hot-press at 150 ᵒC for the optimum vulcanizing times which were determined by a U-CAN UR-2010SD rheometer. The samples are referred to as ZxCy, where x and y denote x and y phr (parts per hundreds of gum) of zinc oxide (ZnO) and CDs, respectively. The specific formulations are listed as SBR 100 phr, stearic acid 2 phr, CZ 1.5 phr, 2, 2′-dithiobis(benzothiazole) (DM) 0.5 phr, sulphur 1.5 phr, CDs, and ZnO variable.

In addition, SBR formulations incorporated with 40 phr silica or carbon black were also prepared. The samples are denoted as SZxCy-Silica and SZxCy-CB, where x and y denote x and y phr of ZnO and CDs, respectively.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed on a Philips Tecnai F20 TEM microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Mutiltechnique Surface Analysis equipped with an Al Kα radiation source. The cryogenically fractured surfaces of rubber samples were observed by a Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy equipped with a HORIBA EX-250 energy dispersive X-ray analyser. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was evaluated with an Agilent 1260 Infinity II LC System HPLC equipped with a variable wavelength detector to quantitatively determined the components of model compounds. Acetonitrile was used as the mobile phase and the flow rate was set at 1 mL/min. All samples were measured at a wavelength of 254 nm. The cross-linking kinetics of rubber samples were recorded at various temperatures by using a U-CAN UR-2010SD rheometer. Dynamic mechanical analysis was performed on a TA Q800 dynamic mechanical analyser in tensile mode with the preset frequency and strain of 1 Hz and 0.5%, respectively. The scanning temperature ranged from −80 to 60 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C/min under purging liquid nitrogen. Rubber samples were subjected to thermal-oxidative aging at 100 °C and at atmospheric pressure for different duration with a Binder M115 heating chambers, following the ISO standard 188:2011. Tensile stress-strain experiments were carried out on a Gotech Al-7000 S servo control system universal testing machine at 25 °C. Dumb-bell test specimens were tested with a deformation rate of 500 mm/min, following the ISO standard 37:2017. For each formulation, six specimens were measured to calculate the average mechanical properties and standard deviations. Equilibrium swelling tests were conducted by immersing the rubber samples in toluene at room temperature for 72 h. The cross-linking densities of the rubber samples were determined using the Flory-Rehner equation [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs as activator on accelerated sulphur cross-linking
ce:section.ce:para: For sulphur cross-linking in diene rubber systems, organic amines are generally known as a promising alterative of ZnO particles, which function as both activator and accelerator by virtue of the secondary/tertiary amine-containing moieties in their chemical structures [16]. As demonstrated by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1), the synthesized CDs exhibits strong absorptions centred at 1650 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 which should be ascribed to the CO stretching and N–H bending vibrations of the amide moieties. Also, the absorption band within a range of 3130–3300 cm−1 confirmed the stretching vibrations of the N–H group in both primary and secondary amine moieties. The deconvoluted high-resolution N 1s spectrum of the synthesized CDs (Fig. S2) also confirms the results of FTIR spectra. Accordingly, we incorporated the synthesized CDs into diene rubber formulations to partially substitute ZnO, and envisage that this biocompatible nanoparticle can potentially regarded as an eco-friendly alternative of toxic ZnO.

Fig. 1 a shows the cross-linking kinetics of the SBR compounds with various CDs loading at 150 °C, and the corresponding cross-linking characteristics are tabulated in Table S1. As expected, the cross-linking rate k of the system significantly accelerated with the increasing CDs loading. For instance, with the incorporation of 3 phr CDs to substitute equivalent ZnO, the Z2C3 sample achieved an increase of approximately 170% in cross-linking rate compared to Z5C0. In general, sulphur cross-linking process can be divided into the following three stages: (1) formation of sulphurating agent, (2) formation of cross-linking precursors, and (3) the formation of cross-links. The first two stages determine the induction time of the process while the last stage indeed involves the formation of sulphur-based networks. Although the mechanism of cross-links formation is still controversial, it is generally accepted that the cross-linking reactions predominately follow a polar pathway in the presence of zinc species [35]. More specifically, the cross-linking precursor can react with active sulphurating agents to generate a zinc-containing precursor, providing a nucleophilic character to the sulphur atoms in the vicinity of the zinc centres. This species can attack the allylic site in rubber chain via an SN2 mechanism, giving rise to the formation of sulphur-based networks. Notably, it has been pointed out that if zinc centres coordinate with amine-containing species, the nucleophilicity of the sulphur atoms in the zinc-containing precursors can be significantly improved [36]. As the abundant N-related moieties with lone electron pairs of CDs can inevitably coordinate with metal ions [37], we envisage that the accelerated cross-linking process in ZxCy system should be attributed to this fact.

Besides, the induction time of the system exhibited a gradual reduction with the increasing CDs loading. As the N-related moieties in CDs can coordinate with zinc complexes, and hence facilitate both the nucleophilic reaction between mercaptide with S8 to form sulphurating agent and its nucleophilic attack of diene rubber chains to form cross-link precursors, which crucially determine the reduction of induction time [1,35]. This phenomenon is conventional in the cases incorporated amine-containing species as accelerators [16].

In general, the direct reduction of ZnO loading will considerably affect the cross-linking density of sulphur-based networks, leading to an inevitable deterioration in the mechanical performance of cross-linked samples. Representative strain-stress curves of the ZxCy samples are shown in Fig. S3a, and the corresponding mechanical properties are reported in Table S2. Obviously, the substitution of CDs for ZnO barely affects the static or dynamic mechanical properties of the system (Figs. 1b and S3b). Even though the ZnO loading was decreased to 40 wt%, the mechanical properties and cross-linking density of Z2C3 sample were almost unchanged compared with those of Z5C0 sample. In addition, amine-passivated CDs has been demonstrated to feature prominent reactive radical scavenging activity, due to the fact that both the surface N-related moieties and energy traps can capture and stabilize the reactive radicals [32,38]. Accordingly, it has been utilized as an efficient antioxidant to improve the thermo-aging resistance of polymers [32]. As shown in Fig. S4, with increasing CDs loading, the retention of stretchability of the samples after thermo-aging revealed a gradual increase, which demonstrated the incorporation of the resultant CDs can significantly improve the thermo-aging resistance of the cross-linked samples.

The above results indicate that the synthesized CDs can serve as an eco-friendly substitute for toxic ZnO to efficiently activate the sulphur vulcanization of diene rubber formulations, providing a green and feasible avenue to substantially alleviate the environmental impact of zinc pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of accelerated sulphur cross-linking activated by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From a mechanistic point of view, temperature-dependent cross-linking kinetics of ZxCy system were recorded and the corresponding cross-linking rates at different temperatures were calculated to extract the activation effect of CDs on sulphur cross-linking process. As shown in Figs. 2a and S5, the cross-linking rate for all the compounds monotonously increase with the temperature (the detailed data are tabulated in Table S3), due to the thermo-activation of sulphur cross-linking reactions. In addition, with increasing CDs loading, the cross-linking rate of the system gradually accelerated in the test temperature range. The corresponding activation energies (Ea) of the sulphur cross-linking reactions in ZxCy compounds were calculated (Fig. 2 b). Notably, the Ea of the system significantly reduces as the CDs loading is increased, implicitly demonstrating that the coordination between the N-related moieties of CDs and the zinc centres in cross-linking precursors can significantly facilitate the nucleophilic reactions between rubber chains and cross-linking precursors to form sulphur-based networks. In addition, it has been observed that organic amines with secondary/tertiary amine-containing moieties can nucleophilically attack the cyclic S8 which undergo an ionic ring-opening reaction to form polysulfides [39,40]. These linear sulphur-containing species are more liable to be thermally activated to generate sulphur radicals which can subsequently induce the cross-linking of diene rubber chains. Hence, it's reasonable to believe that the synthesized CDs can also activate the formation of cross-links upon this pathway.

In order to gain insight into the activation mechanism of cross-linking stage upon the substitution of CDs for ZnO in rubber formulation, model compound experiments were carried out at 150 °C by introducing 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene (TME) as reactive model agent (Table S4). HPLC spectra of the filtered reaction products at different time intervals were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2c, the characteristic peaks centred at the retention times of 3.3, 4.7 and 11.9 min in all the spectra are ascribed to the eluted 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT), N- cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfonamide (CZ) and sulphur, respectively. It can clearly be seen that the amount of CZ and sulphur in the model compound without the addition of CDs (M0) only exhibited a mild decrease even after reacting at 150 °C for 15 min, indicating that the cross-linking process was stranded on the first two stages and the formation of cross-links has not yet been triggered. On the contrary, with the incorporation of 3 phr CDs to substitute equivalent ZnO, a substantial reduction of both CZ and sulphur content in M1 compound was observed only after reacting at 150 °C for 7.5 min, along with considerable development of MBT content. In general, MBT is formed due to both the decomposition of accelerator and the transformation of cross-linking precursors into cross-links. Combined with the cross-linking kinetics of Z2C3 compound 150 °C (Fig. 2a), it is logical to believe that the increase of MBT content in M1 compound at this time should be mainly attributed to the decomposition of CZ. After reacting at 150 °C for 15 min, the corresponding MBT content further increased and visible flocculation related to the cross-linked products can be found in M1 compound (Fig. S6), providing convincing evidence that the incorporation of CDs can activate the cross-linking reactions in M1 compound.

As mentioned above, the N-related moieties with lone electron pairs of CDs are likely to feature firm binding affinity toward the metal centres in zinc-containing species, and hence significantly facilitate the sulphur cross-linking process. To corroborate the interaction between CDs and zinc-containing species, SEM and EDS mapping images of Z2C3 sample were collected. Compared to Z5C0 sample in which legible ZnO particles can be observed, the fracture surface of Z2C3 sample was much rougher with few distinguishable aggregations (Fig. S7). This implied that the interaction between CDs and zinc-containing species can improve the dispersion of residual ZnO particles, which may also promote the activation efficiency of the residual ZnO on sulphur cross-linking process. Furthermore, EDS elemental mapping images revealed that the location of nitrogen-rich region in Z2C3 sample appeared to almost overlap with that of zinc-rich region (Fig. 2d), which directly confirmed the strong interaction between CDs and zinc-containing species.

Hereto, several functions of CDs in different stages of sulphur cross-linking process are discerned as follows. Firstly, the incorporation of CDs can improve the dispersion of ZnO particles in rubber matrix and hence promote the activation efficiency of the residual ZnO on sulphur cross-linking process. Secondly, the coordinative interactions between the N-related moieties of CDs and zinc-containing species facilitate the formation of both sulphurating agent, cross-link precursors and sulphur-based networks. Finally, these N-related moieties can independently attack the cyclic S8 to form liner polysulfides which are liable to convert into sulphur radicals at elevated temperatures and subsequently induce the cross-linking of rubber chains. A plausible mechanism of the accelerated sulphur cross-linking activated by CDs in diene rubber systems is proposed and depicted in Fig. S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs as activator in nano-filled rubber formulations
ce:section.ce:para: From a practical point of view, most of the rubber formulations have to incorporate nano-particles in order to achieve efficient mechanical reinforcement of the matrix. Therefore, the synthesized CDs were incorporated into the rubber formulations filled with conventional fillers (carbon black or silica) to validate the availability of CDs as a versatile activator for the sulphur cross-linked rubber formulations in practice. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the cross-linking rate k of the nano-filled systems (40 phr carbon black or silica) apparently accelerated after incorporating CDs to substitute equivalent ZnO (detailed cross-linking characteristics are tabulated in Table S5). A sight decline of maximum torque is observed in the cross-linking curves of the samples with the incorporation of CDs, this is probably due to the interaction between CDs and fillers is different from that between ZnO and fillers. Moreover, with the incorporation of the synthesized CDs, the cross-linking densities and mechanical properties of the nano-filled samples were almost identical to those of the control samples (Fig. 3b and Fig. S9, detailed cross-linking densities and mechanical properties are reported in Table S6), while the corresponding thermo-aging resistance certainly improved (Fig. S10).

In summary, we report a brand-new strategy to substantially reduce the dosage of toxic ZnO in diene-rubber systems upon incorporating eco-friendly amine-passivated CDs as a green activator for conventional sulphur vulcanization. As the N-related moieties of CDs can coordinate with the zinc centres in cross-linking precursors, the nucleophilic reactivity of the precursors with the allylic sites in rubber chains is significantly improved. Meanwhile, these N-related moieties can directly attack the cyclic S8 to form linear polysulfides which are liable to be thermally activated to generate sulphur radicals. Accordingly, the incorporation of CDs can remarkably reduce the activation energy of the sulphur cross-linking reactions, and hence facilitate the formation of sulphur-based networks in SBR composites. Even in the case that 60 wt% of ZnO was substituted by equivalent CDs, the cross-linking rate of the compound dramatically accelerated while the mechanical performance and cross-linking density of the sample are identical to the unsubstituted one. The activation efficiency of the synthesized CDs on sulphur cross-linking process was also validated in the rubber formulations filled with conventional fillers. We anticipate that the present work can provide a green and practical avenue to substantially abate the environmental pollution from eco-toxic ZnO in rubber industry.

Siwu Wu: Formal analysis, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Chong Xiao: Investigation. Shaoxin Kong: Data curation. Bo Li: Investigation. Zhijun Yang: Resources. Zhenghai Tang: Writing – review & editing. Fang Liu: Validation. Baochun Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: The authors declare the following competing financial interest(s): A Chinese patent based on this work has been filed (No. ZL202010108111. X).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the China National Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists (51825303) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51903085 and 51790503), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640779), and Open Research Fund of CAS Key Laboratory of High-Performance Synthetic Rubber and its Composite Materials, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (HSRCM201901).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on zinc oxide nanoparticles: antibacterial activity and toxicity mechanism

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc-based curing activators: new trends for reducing zinc content in rubber vulcanization Process
